title: Keeping chickens and ducks together
subtitle: What works and what to watch when sharing a house and run between species.
excerpt: Chickens and ducks can share outdoor space without significant problems, but their needs differ enough that some management adjustments are essential. Ducks wet everything; chickens need dry perches. Ducks need deeper water; chickens cannot swim. Getting the shared infrastructure right prevents the common friction points that make mixed flocks more work than they need to be.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Ducks (2010), gov.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018), gov.uk. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: Unwanted sexual behaviour by male ducks directed at hens in a mixed flock. Because chickens are a different species and do not respond to drake mating signals, ducks may attempt to mate with hens in the absence of enough female ducks. This causes feather loss and injury to the hens. The solution is a correct ratio of ducks to drakes, or separating the drake.

Bedding saturated with water from duck drinkers and ducks shaking water from their plumage. Wet litter creates conditions for coccidiosis, bumblefoot from wet floors, and respiratory problems in chickens. Managing water placement and drainage prevents it.

content.text: Chickens and ducks occupy different ecological niches in the farmyard. Chickens scratch and dust-bathe; ducks probe wet ground and dip their heads in water. These different habits mean they compete less than two flocks of the same species would, which is why a mixed yard often works reasonably well in practice. The friction comes from the wet environment ducks create and, in mixed flocks with a drake, from

Ducks need to submerge their heads to clear their nostrils; a bucket or trough at least 20 cm deep meets this need. But they also shake water from their plumage, splash when preening, and knock over any drinker they can reach.
is the primary management challenge in a mixed house.
Place duck water outside or on a concrete section that drains away from the bedded area.
Keep chicken drinkers raised above duck head height (ducks will use them and contaminate the water with beak material).
Check bedding moisture daily; replace visibly wet sections before the whole bed is compromised.

Chickens perch at night; ducks nest on the floor. A house serving both needs to have adequate perch space for the chickens (30 cm per bird minimum) and a section of the floor with clean, deep bedding where ducks can settle. Most standard chicken houses work fine if the floor bedding depth is increased and duck water is kept outside.

Chickens and ducks have different nutritional requirements. Layer pellets formulated for chickens contain calcium levels appropriate for hen eggshell production; ducks on a laying pellet that is too high in calcium can develop kidney problems over time. If the flock includes both laying hens and a laying duck, choose a lower-calcium grower pellet for both and offer oyster shell separately in a feeder the chickens access (ducks rarely eat shell freely). If the ducks are kept for meat rather than eggs, a grower pellet for both is a workable compromise.

A drake in a mixed flock without enough female ducks will attempt to mate with hens. The mating technique differs between species and the physical difference in anatomy means the drake can injure or drown a hen. Keep a ratio of at least one duck to one drake in a mixed flock, and observe behaviour in the first weeks. If hens are losing feathers from their backs or necks, the drake is the cause. A second female duck, or removing the drake from the mixed pen, resolves the problem.

Ducks resist many common chicken pathogens. Red mite, the main external parasite in chicken coops, does not affect ducks. Coccidiosis strains are host-specific, so an outbreak in the chickens will not spread to the ducks. Avian influenza rules apply to both species equally: during an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ), both must be housed whatever their normal management.
